claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 18 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 18 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to ask you a few questions about your child or children.

**First question: How old is your child (or children)?**

The age determines which pathway we'll follow:
- **Under 16**: Generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- **16-19**: May qualify if they're in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Generally not eligible for Child Benefit

Please tell me the age(s) of the child or children you're asking about.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - Alex, who's 18 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! So you have one child, Alex, who is 18 years old.

Since Alex is between 16 and 19 years old, their eligibility will depend on whether they're in approved education or training. Let me check the next step in the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Now I need to know about Alex's current education or training status.

**Is Alex currently:**
1. **In approved education or training**, or
2. **Has recently left education or training** (within the last few months), or
3. **Neither** - not in education/training and hasn't recently left

Which of these applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is currently in approved education - he's studying for his A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since Alex is studying for A-levels, that's approved education. Now I need to check that the level and type of education meets the requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news! A-levels are approved education for Child Benefit purposes. Now I need to verify a few details about Alex's course:

**Questions about Alex's A-level studies:**

1. **Is the course full-time?** (At least 12 hours per week of supervised study - not counting homework or private study time)

2. **Is this course funded by an employer, or is there an agreement that Alex will work for an employer after completing it?**

3. **Is Alex married or in a civil partnership?** (This only matters if they are - married 16-19 year olds must be financially dependent on you to qualify)

Please let me know the answers to these questions.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, it's a full-time course - he's at college doing his A-levels during the week.

No, it's not funded by an employer or anything like that - it's just regular college.

And no, Alex isn't married.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Alex's A-level course meets all the education requirements:
- ✓ A-levels are approved (non-advanced) education
- ✓ Full-time course with sufficient hours
- ✓ Not employer-funded
- ✓ Not married

Now let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Does Alex work less than 24 hours per week (or is he not in paid work at all)?**

For 16-19 year olds in education, they can still qualify for Child Benefit if they're working part-time, but if they work 24 hours or more per week, they wouldn't qualify.

What's Alex's work situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - he's just focused on his studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Alex meets that requirement too - he's not working 24+ hours per week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right?**

Specifically, is he receiving:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA), or
- Universal Credit, or
- Tax credits

in his own name?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex doing an apprenticeship?**

This is important because most apprenticeships disqualify young people from Child Benefit (except for Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales, which are approved).

Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't doing an apprenticeship - he's just doing A-levels at college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is not in an apprenticeship, so that's fine. Now we move on to checking your eligibility as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your eligibility as the claimant. **Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **ANY** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**, or
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme, or
3. **You have pre-settled status** and meet one of these conditions:
   - Working and earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months
   - Actively looking for work (up to 91 days)
   - Have sufficient resources to support yourself and family (and not claiming certain benefits)
   - Studying and have sufficient resources
   - You're a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
4. **You're a Crown servant living abroad** or in certain qualifying countries

Which of these applies to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! You meet the residency requirement as you normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are you responsible for Alex?**

You're considered responsible if **ANY** of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex**, or
2. **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** (paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit toward their care - this includes food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents), or
3. **You pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to your situation with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You're responsible for Alex as you live together.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question: Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- A former partner

If someone else is claiming, only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child, though you can transfer the claim if both parties agree.

Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! No one else is claiming for Alex, so we can continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: What is Alex's current living situation?**

Is Alex:
1. **In hospital** (as an inpatient)?
2. **In care** (looked after by the local authority)?
3. **Fostered** (living with foster carers)?
4. **Living normally** (at home with you)?

Which applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is living normally at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is living at home with you normally.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question: Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

This would be things like the council paying for:
- Alex's housing costs
- Alex's living expenses or care costs

Is the council paying for any of these things for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Let me finalize the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE ✓**

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for Alex. Here's why:

### Alex qualifies because:
- ✓ Alex is 18 years old and in full-time approved education (A-levels)
- ✓ The course is non-advanced education at the appropriate level
- ✓ Alex is not working 24+ hours per week
- ✓ Alex is not receiving benefits in their own right
- ✓ Alex is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship

### You qualify as the claimant because:
- ✓ You live in the UK (Manchester)
- ✓ You live with Alex and are responsible for them
- ✓ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- ✓ The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance

---

## **Next Steps to Claim:**

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay some or all of the benefit back through tax
5. **Payments:** Child Benefit is usually paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

**Apply here:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Do you have any questions about claiming or the High Income Child Benefit Charge?

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and in full-time approved education (A-levels), which qualifies as non-advanced education. Alex is not working 24+ hours per week, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), lives with Alex and is responsible for them, no one else is claiming for Alex, Alex is living at home normally, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 18-year-old child Alex who is studying A-levels full-time at college. The claimant is UK resident, lives with and is responsible for Alex, and no disqualifying factors apply.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 18 years old and in full-time approved education (A-levels), which qualifies as non-advanced education. Alex is not working 24+ hours per week, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), lives with Alex and is responsible for them, no one else is claiming for Alex, Alex is living at home normally, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 18-year-old child Alex who is studying A-levels full-time at college. The claimant is UK resident, lives with and is responsible for Alex, and no disqualifying factors apply.
